Kibo School raised $2 million to offer online STEM degrees to students in Africa. Tesla was sued by ex-employees from its Sparks, Nevada gigafactory over mass layoffs."

PepsiCo CFO Hugh Johnston said that despite price hikes, 'consumers are staying with us,' discussing earnings, inflation, and pricing power.

Research examining default settings and terms offered to minors by TikTok, WhatsApp, and Instagram across 14 countries found that the three platforms do not offer the same level of privacy and safety protections for children across all markets; the research was conducted by Fairplay, a not-for-profit advocating against marketing that targets children. C2 Ventures raised a new fund to invest in the 'dull, dirty, and dangerous' sector, avoiding crypto, Web3, and consumer companies, staying away from Silicon Valley; one portfolio company is a robotics startup that cleans commercial bathrooms. Sundar Pichai told Google employees to 'be more entrepreneurial' and work with 'more hunger,' announcing that Google would slow its hiring pace for the second half of 2022 after bringing on 10,000 workers during Q2.

"Apple's stock market value briefly peaked above $3 trillion in intra-day trading back in January 2022, and the latest gains in the world's most valuable company follow strong rebounds from several tech heavyweights this year. "The Dow outperformed Thursday on strength in blue-chip bank stocks JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs, as all 23 banks passed the Federal Reserve's annual stress test, indicating they would survive a severe recession. "US stocks rallied as the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge—the PCE index—climbed 4.6% in May, down from 4.7% the prior month, signaling further cooling. The Dow surged more than 200 points following the upward GDP revision and the bank stress test results, with stocks shooting higher out of the gate and staying there through the close. "US stocks jumped to close a strong second quarter, with the Nasdaq 100 nearly breaking a previous first-half record with a 32% gain as AI-linked mega-cap stocks continued to rally. Apple just eclipsed a $3 trillion valuation, and comparisons emerged showing the iPhone maker is now bigger than France's economy and India's entire stock market, on pace for $400 billion in annual revenue and $100 billion in profit. Chinese stocks are expected to see modest gains in the second half as investors grapple with how much policy stimulus will turn around a faltering economy. Tesla's stock performance raised questions about whether it reflects the company's financial health. Apple was also noted for using debt reasonably well. As the second half begins, the market's narrow leadership—dominated by a handful of mega-cap tech names—remains a central concern, but the breadth of positive economic signals and the potential for a soft landing have emboldened bulls. "Asian shares were primed to open higher Monday, following positive momentum from Wall Street and signs of moderating US inflation. "All three major US indexes defied recession fears and banking crisis uncertainty to end the first half on a positive note, with the Nasdaq Composite rallying 31.7% for its biggest first-half increase in four decades. All eyes remain on Friday's jobs report for the next clue on the Fed's rate path."

"Wall Street's main indexes fell sharply Thursday in a broad selloff after strong labor data drove up bond yields and fanned fears that the Federal Reserve will be aggressive in raising interest rates. All 11 S&P 500 sectors were in negative territory, with energy and consumer discretionary falling the most. Minutes from the Fed's latest policy meeting showed that some officials wanted to raise rates in mid-June, though they voted unanimously to keep rates steady. Surprisingly strong private hiring data roiled markets, pushing equities down as Treasury yields moved sharply higher. Goldman Sachs warned that a potential recession could send the S&P 500 to 3,400, representing potential downside of more than 20%. Retail traders poured in $1.4 billion per day on average in June, according to Vanda Research, showing persistent retail enthusiasm despite institutional caution.

The Nasdaq had capped off its best first-half rise in 40 years just last week, but the selloff erased some of those gains. As investors digested the Fed minutes and strong labor data, the prospect of further rate hikes—and the potential recession that could follow—dominated sentiment. The broad-based selling suggested that even the most resilient tech names may not be immune to rising rates and slowing growth."

The returns on capital at Amazon.com don't inspire confidence, according to an analysis. Johnson Outdoors shareholders are in the red over three years. QUALCOMM can manage its debt responsibly, but Amazon's capital efficiency raised questions. As the new week begins, the debate over concentration risk versus historical precedent continues. The Magnificent 7's incredible run has made them nearly untouchable, but valuation concerns and the potential for a broadening market are increasingly discussed. "US stocks climbed as more signs of cooling inflation lifted hopes that Fed rate hikes are near an end. However, the earnings season will test whether fundamentals can justify the multiple expansion that has powered the rally. Investors now look to the Fed's July meeting for confirmation that the end of tightening is in sight."

"US stocks gained to extend their win streak as optimism for a soft landing grew, with the Nasdaq and S&P 500 closing at new highs for the year. The Dow led gains Friday as upbeat results from JPMorgan and Wells Fargo lifted big bank stocks, while other major gauges traded little changed. JPMorgan and Wells Fargo both reported higher-than-expected profits, showing no signs of a slowing economy. Insurance giant UnitedHealth Group rallied hard after its results. Bank earnings set a positive tone, showing that higher rates have boosted net interest income without yet causing a credit crunch. The spotlight now turns to Big Tech results, which will determine whether the AI-driven rally can continue or whether valuations have become too stretched. The combination of resilient banks, cooling inflation, and strong consumer sentiment suggests the economy may avoid a hard landing, but the narrow leadership remains a vulnerability."

"US stocks traded mixed but notched a weekly gain as earnings impressed and inflation cooled. The $12 billion inflow into stocks shows that investors are voting with their feet, believing that inflation is under control and the Fed can soon pause. However, the concentration of gains in mega-cap tech remains a concern, and the upcoming earnings season will test whether the rest of the market can catch up. "The stock market has rallied this year, with year-to-date gains near 17% on the S&P 500 and 35% on the NASDAQ. Goldman Sachs analysts expect the stock market to stay 'fat and flat' this year as the economy attempts to navigate a soft landing. Investors are advised to consider five key questions before jumping into the 'Magnificent 7' rally. The dollar's weakness could provide a tailwind for international equities, but the Fed's next move will be critical. "Stock futures lacked momentum Monday after more signs that China's economic recovery is still lagging, as investors prepared for a busy week of earnings reports. "Traders are increasingly confident that the Federal Reserve will hold interest rates steady in December, with CME Group's FedWatch tool showing 90% odds, while pricing in an about 80% chance of the first policy easing coming as soon as June. "The S&P 500 and Nasdaq scored their longest winning streaks in two years, as the Dow ticked up almost two-tenths of a percent and the 10-year Treasury note yield declined for the fifth time in six sessions on expectations that the Fed is done with its rate-hiking cycle. Caution is warranted, but the trend is currently bullish."

"The Nasdaq and S&P 500 extended their longest winning streaks since 2021, eking out gains as investors digested corporate earnings. The Fed's Powell speaks again, and any hawkish tilt could derail the rally. "The Dow climbed more than 1%, the S&P 500 added a percent and a half, and the Nasdaq soared more than 2% for its biggest one-day percentage gain since May, as stocks rallied to close out a winning week. "All S&P 500 sectors posted gains last week, with tech leading at 2.6%, but the new week began with stocks slipping as investors focused on the upcoming October CPI report and Moody's downgrade of the US outlook. Investors should brace for volatility around the CPI release and adjust positions accordingly. The long-term trend remains positive, but short-term risks are elevated.”

Overall, the tone was constructive, but it was tempered by reminders that stock picking, valuation discipline, and insider behavior remain critical in a market that could still experience sharp swings."

Overall, the market tone remained constructive, but the day showed that gains were being balanced by profit taking, holiday thin liquidity, and ongoing scrutiny of how quickly policy may shift from holding rates steady to easing."

"Equity markets ended the week with a mix of optimism and caution as analysts continued to point to a strong 2024 outlook for U.S. stocks. Individual stock stories showed the wide spread of outcomes across sectors, from strong gains in some names to weaker long term returns in others. Elsewhere, investors continued to compare stock picking with passive investing, with several articles noting that index funds remain a simple way to capture market gains without the risk of picking individual winners and losers. "Attention shifted to the week ahead as investors prepared for the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation reading, which was set to be the main test for the market rally. "Oil advanced after a six-week run of losses on speculation that the Federal Reserve is done raising interest rates, while US sanctions on Venezuelan supply could be tightened again. The debate now turns to how low the Fed will need to go with rate cuts, as traders are convinced the hiking cycle is over. The November jobs report will be a key test for the market's soft landing narrative. Meanwhile, the TikTok trend #BookTok has 'literally changed my life,' altering the publishing industry.
